description: VERIS taxonomy for describing cybersecurity incidents, including categories and enumerated values.
name: VERIS Framework

values.description: Misdelivery (send wrong info or to wrong recipient)
Programming error (flaws or bugs in custom code). This is the error equivalent of the hacking/malware 'Exploit vuln' enumerations.
Publishing error (private info to public doc or site)
Carelessness. An honest mistake due to inattention to detail. Use it when it is known to be so.
The hacking action resulted in additional security permissions
The hacking action exfiltrated data from the victim
The hacking action resulted in additional security access
The hacking action used security access or permissions already acuired
The hacking action did not have a result
The result of the hacking action is not listed
The action resulted in some form of persistence on the system
The result of the hacking action is unknown
Hacking action that creates a backdoor for use.
Brute force or password guessing attacks.
Cross-site request forgery. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Disable or interfere with security controls
Modification of the action (rather than the system, as in 'Disable controls') to avoid detection.
Exploit a misconfiguration (vs vuln or weakness)
Exploit vulnerability in code (vs misconfig or weakness). This can be used with other hacking enumerations, (such as XSS when an XSS vuln exists.). Parent of many hacking varieties.
Forced browsing or predictable resource location. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Format string attack. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
HTTP request smuggling. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
HTTP request splitting. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
HTTP response smuggling. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
HTTP response splitting. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
To assume control over and steal functionality for an illicit purpose (e.g. Hijacking phone number intercept SMS verification codes)
iterating over sequential or obvious values. https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Top_10-2017_A8-Insecure_Deserialization. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Mail command injection. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Null byte injection. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Offline password or key cracking (e.g., rainbow tables, Hashcat, JtR)
Enumerating the state of the current host
Crafting malicious or misleading prompts to bypassing safety filters and executing unintended instructions in LLMs
Remote file inclusion. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Enumerating the state of the network
Credential or session prediction. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Soap array abuse. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Special element injection. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
URL redirector abuse. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Use of stolen or default authentication credentials (including credential stuffing)
Elevation of privilege by another customer in shared environment. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Virtual machine escape. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
XML attribute blowup. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
XML entity expansion. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
XML external entities. Child of 'Exploit vuln'.
Hacking actions taken through a backdoor.  C2 is only used by malware.
Graphical desktop sharing (RDP, VNC, PCAnywhere, Citrix)
Superset of 'Desktop sharing' and '3rd party desktop'.  Please use in place of the other two
Penetration of another VM or web site on shared device or infrastructure
Network service that is not remote access or a web application.
Partner connection or credential. (Indicates supply chain breach.)
Physical access or connection (i.e., at keyboard or via cable)
The malware action resulted in additional security permissions
The malware action exfiltrated data from the victim
The malware action resulted in additional security access
The malware action used security access or permissions already acuired
The malware action did not have a result
The result of the malware action is not listed
The action resulted in some form of persistence on the system
The result of the malware action is unknown
System or network utilities (e.g., PsTools, Netcat)
Malware creates a backdoor capability for hacking. Child of 'RAT' when combined with 'Trojan'. Child of 'Backdoor or C2'.
Malware creates a remote control capability, but it's unclear if it's a backdoor for hacking or C2 for malware. Parent of 'C2' and 'Backdoor'.
Malware creates Command and Control capability for malware. Child of 'Backdoor or C2'.
Capture data from application or system process
Capture data stored on system disk
Click fraud, whether or not cryptocurrency mining.  Also mark 'Click fraud or cryptocurrency mining'. Child of 'Click fraud and cryptocurrency mining'.
Click fraud or cryptocurrency mining. Parent of 'Click fraud' and 'Cryptocurrency mining'.
Client-side or browser attack (e.g., redirection, XSS, AitB)
Cryptocurrency mining, whether or not click fraud. Child of 'Click fraud and cryptocurrency mining'.
Disable or interfere with security controls
Modification of the action (rather than the system, as in 'Disable controls') to avoid detection.
Exploit a misconfiguration (vs vuln or weakness)
Exploit vulnerability in code (vs misconfig or weakness). This can be used with other malware enumerations, (such as Remote injection when a Remote injection vuln exists.)
Export data to another site or system
Malware which compromises a legitimate file rather than creating new filess
Enumerating the state of the current host
RAM scraper or memory parser (capture data from volatile memory)
Remote Access Trojan.  Parent of 'Backdoor' and 'Trojan'
Enumerating the state of the network
Spyware, keylogger or form-grabber (capture user input or activity)
An application which appears legitimate but hides malicious functionality. Child of 'RAT' when combined with 'Backdoor'
Worm (propagate to other systems or devices)
Malware actions taken through a Command and Control (C2) channel.  Backdoor is only used by hacking.
Directly installed or inserted by threat agent (after system access)
Downloaded and installed by local malware
Email. Parent to 'Email attachment', 'Email autoexecute', 'Email link', 'Email unknown'
Email via user-executed attachment. Child of 'Email'
Email via automatic execution. Child of 'Email'
Email via embedded link. Child of 'Email'
Email sub-variety known, but not one of those listed (attachment, link, autoexecute, etc). Child of 'Email'
Email but sub-variety (attachment, autoexecute, link, etc) not known. Child of 'Email'
Partner connection or credential. (Indicates supply chain breach.)
Remotely injected by agent (i.e. via SQLi)
Web application. Parent of 'Web application - download' and 'Web application - drive-by.
Web via user-executed or downloaded content. Child of 'Web application'.
Web via auto-executed or "drive-by" infection. Child of 'Web application'.
Do not use. Misuse inherently implies having permission so none can be elevated.
The misuse action exfiltrated data from the victim
Do not use. Misuse inherently implies having permission so none can be gained.
The misuse action used security access or permissions already acuired
The misuse action did not have a result
The result of the misuse action is not listed
The action resulted in some form of persistence on the system
The result of the misuse action is unknown
Handling of data in an unapproved manner
Inappropriate use of email or IM
Modification of the action (rather than the system, as in 'Disable controls') to avoid detection.
Storage or distribution of illicit content
Abuse of private or entrusted knowledge
Inappropriate use of network or Web access including cloud services
Sharing of a legitimate user's credential or session
Abuse of physical access to asset
Actor photographs the confidentiality data variety.
Use of unapproved hardware or devices
Use of unapproved software or services
Local network access within corporate facility
Remote access connection to corporate network (i.e. VPN)
The physical action resulted in additional security permissions
The physical action exfiltrated data from the victim
The physical action resulted in additional security access
The physical action used security access or permissions already acuired
The physical action did not have a result
The result of the physical action is not listed
The action resulted in some form of persistence on the system
The result of the physical action is unknown
Assault (threats or acts of physical violence)
Modification of the action (rather than the system, as in 'Disable controls') to avoid detection.
Snooping (sneak about to gain info or access)
Victim public or customer area (e.g., lobby, storefront)
Victim high security area (e.g., server room, R&D labs)
Victim private or work area (e.g., office space)
The social action resulted in additional security permissions
The social action exfiltrated data from the victim
The social action resulted in additional security access
The social action used security access or permissions already acuired
The social action did not have a result
The result of the social action is not listed
The action resulted in some form of persistence on the system
The result of the social action is unknown
End-user of the victim's products and/or services. Child of 'End-user or employee'
End-user or regular employee not otherwise listed. Parent of 'End-user' or 'Other employee'
Senior staff with legal responsibility such as board members and corporate officers
Regular employee not otherwise listed. Child of 'End-user or employee'
Prepare malicious content in a location where a victim is likely to interact with it. (e.g. SEO - vect: websites, left usbs- vect: removable media, etc)
Elicitation (subtle extraction of info through conversation)
Modification of the action (rather than the system, as in 'Disable controls') to avoid detection.
Forgery or counterfeiting (fake hardware, software, documents, etc)
Influence tactics (Leveraging authority or obligation, framing, etc)

Any type of *ishing.  Phishing always involves getting data from the victim. Phishing usually has some element of pretexting, but often it doesn’t rise to the level of an invented scenario. E.g. A fake google login page isn’t really pretexting.
Pretexting (dialogue leveraging invented scenario).  Unlike 'Phishing', does not transfer data. (A fraudulent transfer or changing a bank account on a business account is not really disclosing data.
Bombarding the user with MFA prompts to get them to accept the login request
Online scam or hoax (e.g., scareware, 419 scam, auction fraud)
Spam (unsolicited or undesired email and advertisements)
Partner connection or credential. (Indicates supply chain breach.)
The environmental action resulted in additional security permissions
The environmental action exfiltrated data from the victim
The environmental action resulted in additional security access
The environmental action used security access or permissions already acuired
The environmental action did not have a result
The result of the environmental action is not listed
The action resulted in some form of persistence on the system
The result of the environmental action is unknown

Any criminal(s) or group with a level of organization
Fired, laid off, or let go
Senior staff with legal responsibility such as board members and corporate officers

Embedded -  Variety known but not listed
Embedded - A dedicated device that affects the real world
Embedded - A dedicated device that collects data about the physical world
Media - The output of a fax machine
Media - Variety known but not listed
Media  - Subscriber Identity Module found in mobile devices
Network - Access control reader (e.g., badge, biometric)
Network - Variety known but not listed
Asset type known but not User Device, Server, Public Terminal, Server, People, Network, or Media
People - End-user of the victim's products and/or services. Child of 'P - End-user or employee'
Person - End-user or regular employee not otherwise listed. Parent of 'P - End-user' or 'P - Other employee'
People - Senior staff with legal responsibility such as board members and corporate officers
People - Variety known but not listed
Person - Regular employee not otherwise listed. Child of 'P - End-user or employee'
Servers maintaining or deploying configurations or patches to other assets
Server - Industrial Control System (ICS).  Includes Supervisory Control And Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems.
Server - Large Language Model application, including chatbots and custom coded applications
Server - Variety known but not listed
Server - Secrets vault used to store passwords for use in the environment
Public Terminal - Variety known but not listed
Public Terminal - Detached PIN pad or card reader
User Device - Authentication token or device
User Device - Desktop or workstation. Child to 'U - Desktop or laptop'
User Device - Desktop, workstation, or laptop.  Parent to 'U - Desktop' and 'U - Laptop'
User Device - Laptop. Child to 'U - Desktop or laptop'
User Device - Media player or recorder
User Device - Mobile phone or smartphone
User Device - Variety known but not listed
User Device - Peripheral (e.g., printer, copier, fax)
(cloud.Yes) Cloud assets were involved in the incident
(cloud.No) Cloud is not applicable to this incident. Only use if computer's weren't involved (people, media, etc). Do not use for Desktops or other user assets.
On premise (including colo) assets were involved in the incident.
Cloud hosting known but not listed
The involvement of cloud assets was not measured
South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands
Macedonia, The former Yugoslav Republic of
A third party provider hosted the affected asset on servers dedicated to the victim organization.

A third party provider hosted the affected asset on a shared physical device. This is common in web hosting services where multiple organizations’ web applications are resident on the same physical server.
A third party provided hosted the affected asset, but it is unknown whether the victim organization’s environment was shared or dedicated.
The affected asset was hosted within the victim organization’s location.
Hosting label is not applicable, a Human asset that clicks a malicious attachment is a common example as is portable assets like laptops or paper documents.
Hosting arrangement known but not listed
The hosting arrangement of the asset was not known to the incident responder or not recorded.
The affected asset was managed and maintained by both internal administrators as well as a third party provider or partner. There is a level of shared responsibility.
The affected asset was managed and maintained by a third party service provider or business partner.
The affected asset was managed and maintained by employees of the victim organization.
Management label is not applicable, a Human asset that clicks a malicious attachment is a common example.
The responsibility for management and maintenance of the affected asset was not known to the incident responder or not recorded.
The affected assets is consumer owned – an example would be credentials stolen from a banking customer then reused to access a banking application
The asset was owned by an employee of the victim organization. BYOD assets would be categorized under this label. Currently
Ownership label is not applicable, a Human asset that clicks a malicious attachment is a common example.
The asset (regardless of physical location) was the property of a business partner, including a cloud provider.
Ownership of the asset was not known to the incident responder or not recorded.
The affected asset is property of the victim organization
The concept of a role is not applicable to the asset.
Known but not IT or OT
Application Programing Interface key are used for programmatic access to applications, if unsure, use Credentials
Authentication credentials, such as username and password
Sensitive internal data (e.g., plans, reports, emails)
Any additional authentication methods, aside from Username and password (e.g., OTP, biometrics)
Payment card data (e.g., PAN, PIN, CVV2, Expiration)
Personal or identifying information (e.g., addr, Email address, credit score)
Data which impacts a person's livelihood, quality of life, and ability to participate in daily activities such as SSNs or other National ID
Session keys associated with an existing connection and used to identify the client, if unsure, use Credentials
System information (e.g., config info, open services)
The data was that of the victim organization rather than a customer, employee, etc.
Data state known but not listed.
Stored (either encrypted, unencrypted, or where encryption is unknown.
compromise of authenticity (e.g. masquerading as the legitimate owner of an account)
Modified how a user account is authenticated, such as removing MFA device
Register an attacker controlled MFA device to an account
On-disk software installation or code modification
disclosed by threat agent (e.g., public brag, private blackmail)
reported by customer or partner affected by the incident
notified by law enforcement or government agency
Discovery method was external and known but not listed including related 3rd parties
security researcher attempting to identify insecure data
Discovered by person unaffiliated with victim or threat actor
employee discovered evidence of a break in
host IDS or file integrity monitoring
discovered while responding to another (separate) incident
Health and welfare monitoring of assets such as utilization, uptime, and SNMP alerts
Any routine maintenance, testing or review of it assets. (Includes inspect of assets, vulnerability scans, etc.)
Use when the incident is discovered during the exit interview or offboarding/decommissioning of an employee.
Discovery method was internal and known but not listed
reported by employee who saw something odd
Notified by antivirus company but not through AV product
Audit performed by a partner organization
Discovery method was partner and known but not listed
Asset (including physical currency) and fraud-related losses
Impact variety known but not listed.
Catastrophic: A business-ending event (don't choose this if the victim will continue operations)
Damaging: Real and serious effect on the "bottom line" and/or long-term ability to generate revenue
Distracting: Limited "hard costs", but impact felt through having to deal with the incident rather than conducting normal duties
Insignificant: Impact absorbed by normal activities
Painful: Moderate "hard costs", and impact felt through having to deal with the incident rather than conducting normal duties has quantifiable indirect costs
Data was not misused or abused
Neither 'yes' nor 'no' describes data abuse in this case.
No information is known about if the data was abused
Data was ransomed by the threat actor, child of Yes
The data was directly used for financial fraud (ie using stolen payment cards), child of Yes
The data was used stealing individuals identity, child of Yes
The data was posted on a personal blog post or social media, child of Yes
False positive (response triggered, but no incident)
Near miss (actions did not compromise asset)
Not applicable.  Use when actor does not have a practical choice of targets or no intent (i.e. Error).
Opportunistic: victim attacked because they exhibited a weakness the actor knew how to exploit
Targeted: victim chosen as target then actor determined what weaknesses could be exploited
Compromise does not apply in the context of the security event.
Containment does not apply in the context of the security event.
Discovery does not apply in the context of the security event.
Exfiltration does not apply in the context of the security event.
Recieve value meant for someone else, (such as redirected affiliate proceeds or click fraud
No type of cash-out was necessary
The variety of cash-out was known, but is not listed
See distribution and non-distribution services for potential examples
Purchase physical goods as a means of transferring a compromised account's content or currency to the actor.
likely the data.variety for breaches or asset for stolen assets
Nothing is known about the need for or type of cash-out investment other than it was present.
A small program that can be distributed, installed, and controlled en mass.
Develop an email such as for phishing.
Code to exploit a vulnerability, including web injects.
Code sets capable of selecting and trying multiple exploits against a target.
No type of development was necessary
The variety of development required is known, but is not listed.
The portion a program that causes a negative effect.
A fake representation of a person, such as fake social media profiles
Development of something physical such as a skimming device
A program which masquerades as another program to get a target to execute malicious content
Nothing is known about the need for or type of development investment other than it was present.
Development of any full website controlled by the attacker
For content distributed from a collection of bots.
malicious content added to a benign server, such as a webserver, by the actor, without the permission or necessarily knowledge of the server’s owner
Distributed directly from the actor's computer
Distribution by email including anonymous/one time and spam
No type of distribution was necessary
The variety of distribution was known, but is not listed
The actor distributed the attack to the victim through a partner.  (Indicates supply chain breach.)
Distribution over the Plain Old Telephone System (POTS).
Nothing is known about the need for or type of distribution investment other than it was present.
Malicious content shared intentionally, including bullet-proof hosting
Trade-based, shell companies, under/over paying, cash-intensive businesses, etc, used for money laundering
Breaking an amount of cryptocurrency into smaller amounts and quickly filtering it through multiple accounts
An individual believes they are legitimately employed, and launders money/items during the course of their employment.
Gambling related money laundering. (e.g. Converting to chips and back or big wins and losses.
No type of money laundering was necessary
The variety of money laundering was known, but is not listed

Individuals receive packages purchased through fraudulent means and subsequently repackage the merchandise for shipment, usually abroad.
likely paired with value_chain.cash-out.variety.Purchase stolen goods
Breaking non-crypt currency up into smaller amounts to transfer
Nothing is known about the need for or type of money laundering other than it was present.
Command and control. Separate from distribution of mawlare or bots, this is how they are maintained
Services for testing if malware is detected by anti-virus
Something kept in the custody of a third party until a condition has been fulfilled.
i.e. converting hashes into the text that produce them
Use of a marketplace was required as part of this incident.
No type of non-distribution service was necessary
The variety of non-distribution service required is known, but is not listed
A proxy service (either formally or informally hosted) is used by the actor to obscure their source

lost or stolen credentials, including credental stuffing, used to pick an organization as a target
Knowledge of system misconfigurations used to pick an organization as a target

Information on an organization such as org chart, technologies in use, financial assets, etc, used to pick them as a target
The variety of targeting was known, but is not listed
The actor used access to a partner to target the victim.
Information on individuals such as title, interests, physical location, etc, used to pick an organization as a target
Targeting through physical means such as geographic location.
